title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Searching method is used to locate intervals where a root is hidden before using more precise methods like Bisection or Newton-Raphson.
Important idea: We are looking for a sign change in the function. If \(f(x)\) goes from negative to positive (or vice versa), the curve must have crossed the x-axis.

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Searching method is used to locate intervals where a root is hidden before using more precise methods like Bisection or Newton-Raphson.
Important idea: We are looking for a sign change in the function. If \(f(x)\) goes from negative to positive (or vice versa), the curve must have crossed the x-axis.

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Bisección
La idea es simple: si \(f(a)\) y \(f(b)\) tienen signos opuestos, hay una raíz entre \(a\) y \(b\). Dividimos el intervalo a la mitad repetidamente.

import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

def biseccion(f, a, b, tol=1e-6, max_iter=100):
    historial = []
    for i in range(max_iter):
        c = (a + b) / 2
        historial.append({"iteración": i+1, "a": a, "b": b, "c": c, "f(c)": f(c)})
        if abs(f(c)) < tol or (b - a) / 2 < tol:
            break
        if f(a) * f(c) < 0:
            b = c
        else:
            a = c
    return c, pd.DataFrame(historial)

# Función de prueba: f(x) = x³ - x - 2
f = lambda x: x**3 - x - 2

raiz, df = biseccion(f, 1, 2)
print(f"Raíz encontrada: {raiz:.6f}")
df.head(10)

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Convergencia

fig = px.line(
    df,
    x="iteración",
    y="f(c)",
    title="Convergencia — Método de Bisección",
    markers=True,
    labels={"f(c)": "f(c)", "iteración": "Iteración"}
)
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dash", line_color="red", annotation_text="y=0")
fig.show()

                            
                                            
Convergencia del método de bisección

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Newton-Raphson
Usa la derivada para converger más rápido: \(x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\)

def newton_raphson(f, df, x0, tol=1e-6, max_iter=100):
    historial = []
    x = x0
    for i in range(max_iter):
        fx = f(x)
        historial.append({"iteración": i+1, "x": x, "f(x)": fx})
        if abs(fx) < tol:
            break
        x = x - fx / df(x)
    return x, pd.DataFrame(historial)

f  = lambda x: x**3 - x - 2
df_dx = lambda x: 3*x**2 - 1

raiz_nr, df_nr = newton_raphson(f, df_dx, x0=1.5)
print(f"Raíz encontrada: {raiz_nr:.6f}")
df_nr

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Comparación

df["método"] = "Bisección"
df_nr["método"] = "Newton-Raphson"
df["error"] = df["f(c)"].abs()
df_nr["error"] = df_nr["f(x)"].abs()

comparacion = pd.concat([
    df[["iteración", "error", "método"]],
    df_nr[["iteración", "error", "método"]]
])

fig = px.line(
    comparacion,
    x="iteración",
    y="error",
    color="método",
    title="Convergencia: Bisección vs Newton-Raphson",
    markers=True,
    log_y=True
)
fig.show()

                            
                                            
Bisección vs Newton-Raphson

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Theoretical Basis
We are going to support our analysis in Bolzano’s theorem:
Let \(f\) be a continuous function on a closed interval \([a, b]\). If \(f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0\), then:
\[\exists \, c \in (a, b) \text{ such that } f(c) = 0\]
This theorem is so powerful because it guarantees that there’s at least one root in \([a, b]\).

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Hands on
This code is made for applying the searching algorithm to find the roots in quadratic equations, the idea is to show the diference between the analytic solution and the aproximation interval. It is important to mention that this is a modified version. Not only do it works for positive intervals but also for negative ones.


See implementation
import pandas as pd

# Parameters
a = 1.0
b = -3.0
c = 2.0
deltax = 0.1

def searching(roots, step):
  intervalos = []
  historial = []
  x = 0.0
  iterations = 0
  flag = 0
  while flag != roots:
    cuadraticfuncion = lambda a1, b1, c1, x1 : a1*pow(x1, 2) + b1*x1 + c1
    cuadraticfuncionn = lambda a1, b1, c1, x1 : a1*pow(-x1, 2) + b1*(-x1) + c1
    fi_p = cuadraticfuncion(a, b, c, x)
    fi_n = cuadraticfuncionn(a, b, c, x)
    x = x + step
    fi_1_p = cuadraticfuncion(a, b, c, x)
    fi_1_n = cuadraticfuncionn(a, b, c, x)
    if bool(fi_p<0):
      f1 = -1
    else:
      f1 = 1
    if bool(fi_1_p<0):
      f2 = -1
    else:
      f2 = 1
    if bool(fi_n<0):
      f3 = -1
    else:
      f3 = 1
    if bool(fi_1_n<0):
      f4 = -1
    else:
      f4 = 1
    if f1*f2 < 0:
      flag += 1
      xi_1p = x
      xip = x - step
      intervalos.append([xip, xi_1p])
      historial.append({"Iterations": iterations, "xi": round(xip,4), "xi+1": round(xi_1p,4), "f(xi)": round(fi_p,4), "f(xi+1)": round(fi_1_p,4), "Raíz": "Found in Positive side"})
    else:
      xi_1p = x
      xip = x - step
      historial.append({"Iterations": iterations, "xi": round(xip,4), "xi+1": round(xi_1p,4), "f(xi)": round(fi_p,4), "f(xi+1)": round(fi_1_p,4), "Raíz": "Not Found"})
    if f3*f4 < 0:
      flag += 1
      xi_1n = -1 * x
      xin = xi_1n + step
      intervalos.append([xin, xi_1n])
      historial.append({"Iterations": iterations, "xi": round(xin,4), "xi+1": round(xi_1n,4), "f(xi)": round(fi_n,4), "f(xi+1)": round(fi_1_n,4), "Raíz": "Found in Negative side"})
    else:
      xi_1n = -1 * x
      xin = xi_1n + step
      historial.append({"Iterations": iterations, "xi": round(xin,4), "xi+1": round(xi_1n,4), "f(xi)": round(fi_n,4), "f(xi+1)": round(fi_1_n,4), "Raíz": "Not found"})
    
    iterations += 1

  print("¡Great! We have found all roots!")
  print("Intervalos:", intervalos)
  return historial

# Ejecución
print(f"f(x) = {a}x² + {b}x + {c}")
discriminant = pow(b,2) - (4*a*c)
print(f"Discriminant: {discriminant}")

if discriminant < 0:
    print("There aren't real roots")
elif discriminant == 0:
    historial = searching(1, deltax)
else:
    historial = searching(2, deltax)


f(x) = 1.0x² + -3.0x + 2.0
Discriminant: 1.0
¡Great! We have found all roots!
Intervalos: [[0.9999999999999999, 1.0999999999999999], [1.9000000000000004, 2.0000000000000004]]

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
The algorithm
Let be i the iteration, if a sequence of points is defined as follows:
\[x_{i+1} = x_i + \Delta x \]
We can evaluate the crossing condition at each step:
\[f(x_i) \cdot f(x_{i+1}) < 0\]
If this is true, the root is in the interval:
\[(x_i, x_{i+1})\]
If not, \(f>0\), we continue “walking” \(\Delta x\)

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
The error
In Incremental Search, we do not obtain the exact value of the root, but rather a confidence interval. If we find a sign change between \(x_i\) and \(x_{i+1}\), the maximum possible error (the maximum distance to the real root) is the step size:
\[E_{max} \approx \Delta x\]

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text: Visualization

title: Lecture 1.1 Root Finding Methods: Searching Method
text:
Conclusions
The iterative search method relies entirely on the fact that \(Δ𝑥\) is small enough to capture the “frequency” of the function. This means that, if we aren’t careful, we might find roots, but the calculations would take forever if the root were actually far from zero.
